title: Reranking Cost-Benefit
concept: The added latency and compute cost of reranking weighed against the accuracy gain it delivers.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about measuring reranking's impact. What is the primary benefit of reranking?

options.text: To translate documents into multiple languages.
To bring the most relevant documents to the top of the search results.
To delete duplicate records from the vector database.

exercises.explain: The main goal of reranking is to reorder the initially retrieved documents so that the most relevant ones are moved to the top of the list, improving user experience and LLM context quality.

cards.title: The Cost of Precision
cards.body: Retrieving documents is lightning fast, but is it always accurate? Adding a second-stage reranker acts like a fine-toothed comb, but this extra precision isn't free—it costs time and money.

cards.title: Cost-Benefit Analysis
cards.body: Reranking cost-benefit analysis is the process of weighing the extra millisecond latency and computational cost of running a reranker model against the increase in retrieval accuracy. It helps developers decide if the performance boost justifies the extra infrastructure overhead.

cards.title: The Two-Pass Translator
cards.body: Think of reranking like hiring an expert translator to double-check a fast machine translation. The first pass is cheap and fast, while the expert review takes more time and money but ensures the final result is perfectly accurate.

cards.title: E-Commerce Search Trade-off
cards.body: In an e-commerce search engine, adding a reranker increases search latency by 80 milliseconds and costs an extra $0.05 per 1,000 queries. However, it increases the click-through rate on the top 3 results by 15%, making the minor latency hit highly profitable.

cards.title: The Latency Myth
cards.body: Many developers think adding a reranker will make their system slow. In reality, because you only rerank a small pool (like the top 25 documents) rather than millions, the latency impact is usually under 100 milliseconds.

cards.title: Speed vs. Accuracy
cards.body: Compare the differences between using a fast vector search alone versus adding a precise reranker.

left.text: Very fast (milliseconds), cheap compute, but relevant documents might be buried down the list.

right.text: Slightly slower, higher compute cost, but ensures the best documents are boosted to the very top.

exercises.prompt: What is the main trade-off or downside of introducing a reranker to your RAG pipeline?

options.text: It reduces the size of the vector database.
It introduces extra latency and computational cost.
It removes the need for embedding models entirely.
It guarantees that retrieval will take zero milliseconds.

exercises.explain: Because a reranker is a deep learning model that calculates attention scores between the query and each document, it requires extra computational power (CPU/GPU) and time, which increases search latency.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To rerank search results, the reranker model must process every single document in the database.
exercises.explain: Rerankers are only applied to the small subset of documents returned by the initial search (e.g., the top 20 or 50 documents), not the entire database of millions of documents.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about the performance trade-offs of rerankers.
exercises.sentence: Running a reranker model on retrieved documents adds latency to the system response time.
exercises.explain: Latency refers to the delay or time delay introduced by processing steps. Adding a reranker increases latency because the model must perform extra computations on the retrieved documents.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about reranking trade-offs is likely AI-generated nonsense?

options.text: We saw a 10% increase in Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), but it added 50ms of latency per query.
Since our reranking step runs on a self-hosted GPU, we pay more in hosting fees but avoid API per-token costs.
Our reranker compresses document text into binary files, completely bypassing LLM input limits and eliminating all server costs.

exercises.explain: Option C shows a typical AI-generated hallucinated or illogical claim: rerankers do not compress text to bypass LLM limits, nor do they run on the client side to eliminate server costs. Options A and B are standard technical facts.

exercises.prompt: When designing a production RAG system, how should you evaluate the trade-offs of adding a reranking step?

options.text: Rerankers process the entire database, making them cheaper than vector database index updates.
Rerankers always reduce latency by filtering out documents before the vector search database is queried.
Rerankers add processing time and computational cost, but only over a small subset of retrieved documents to improve final relevance.
Rerankers eliminate the need for an LLM generation step, saving overall pipeline compute costs.

exercises.explain: Adding a reranker introduces extra API or hardware costs and increases response latency because it runs a deep neural network on the top-retrieved documents. However, this extra cost is only applied to a small subset (e.g., top 25 docs) rather than the entire database, balancing accuracy and efficiency.
